rows.Name: Osteomyelitis
rows.Description: Severe infection of the bone, marrow, and surrounding tissue
rows.Etiology: Most commonly staphylococcus aureus through direct or indirect infection (indirect more common in pediatrics)
Risk Factors: Open wounds, recent surgery, pressure injuries
Pathophysiology: 4. Dead bone separates and becomes sequestra (island surrounded by purulent drainage)
Treatment: IV antibiotics based on cultures, 4 - 6 weeks (sometimes up to 3 to 6 months)

rows.Name: Osteoporosis
Etiology: 1st degree / primary - occurs in women after menopause and men later in life, result of failure to develop optimal peak bone mass earlier in life
2nd degree / secondary - result of medications or other conditions that affect bone metabolism (e.g. hyperparathyroidism)
Risk Factors: Medical conditions that reduce calcium absorption
Pathophysiology: Osteoblasts (bone creation) can't keep up with osteoclasts (bone destruction)
Diagnostics: DEXA (Dual Energy X-ray Absorptiometry) bone density scan
Quantitative ultrasound (measures density in heel, kneecap, or shin)
Clinical Manifestations: Loss of height (spinal compression and kyphosis)
Treatment: Hormone replacement therapy - must be weighed against risks
Calcitonin IM or intranasal - inhibits osteoclasts (give with calcium supplement to prevent secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Bisphosphonates - bind to bone and poison osteoclasts (take with water, remain upright for 30 min)

rows.Name: Osteoarthritis
rows.Description: "Wear and tear". Cartilage wears down over time until bone-on-bone contact.
Pathophysiology: Cartilage goes from smooth, white, translucent -> dull, yellow, granular
Diagnostics: CBC, renal function, LFTs to rule out other causes
Management: Weight reduction to reduce joint strain
Mild pain - acetaminophen, NSAID (celecoxib), topical analgesic
Moderate pain - intra-articular corticosteroid injections, hyaluronate injection, supportive devices, tramadol, duloxetine, colchicine
Intractable pain / loss of function - opioids, surgery

rows.Name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
rows.Description: Chronic systemic autoimmune disorder, inflammation of connective tissue in synovial joints. Periods of inflammation and remission. Flare-ups can affect other areas.
rows.Etiology: Unknown, likely combination of environmental and genetic factors
Pathophysiology: 1. Antigen triggers the formation of abnormal IgG
2. Antibodies form against IgG known as rheumatoid factor
3. IgG and RF join to form inflammation in joints
4. Neutrophils go to inflamed area, inflammation causes synovial lining to thicken
Diagnostics: CRP, ESR, ANA titres, anti-citrullinated protein antibody
Clinical Manifestations: Leads to deformity and disability: Ulnar drift (fingers towards pinky), Boutonniere deformity (middle finger joint stays flexed), Hallux valgus (bunion), Swan neck (middle finger joint hyperextended, outer joint flexed)
Sjogren syndrome - severe dry eyes and mouth (immune system destroys moisture glands)
Felty syndrome - splenomegaly and low WBC
Treatment: DMARDs (methotrexate [marrow suppression, hepatotoxicity], sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, leflunomide)
TNF drugs (also a DMARD, block TNF-α protein to reduce inflammation) - adalimumab, etanercept
Management: Hospitalization for systemic complications if necessary

rows.Name: Soft Tissue Injury
Description: Strain - overstretching of muscle or tendon (muscle to bone)
Sprain - injury of ligament (bone to bone)
Management: Limit movement, but prolonged rest is not necessary
Notes: Sprains may result in an avulsion fracture where ligament pulls away a piece of bone

rows.Name: Fractures
rows.Description: Bone is broken, typically by trauma
6 Ps Assessment: Poikilothermia - coolness or inability to regulate temperature
Neurovascular Assessment: Extremities: color, temperature, pulse, edema, sensation, strength
Clinical Manifestations: Inability to bear weight or use affected part
May not have obvious bone deformity
Healing Process: 3. Callus forms (contains calcium, phosphorus, magnesium) - ossification begins
4. Ossification occurs 3 weeks to 6 months post fracture, visible on X-ray, mobility still limited
6. Remodeling - can take up to a year for the bone to heal completely
